executive_summary.headline: Analysis of 3,212,243 Government Procurement Contracts (2011–2026)
executive_summary.paragraph_1: This dataset covers 3,212,243 awarded contracts spanning 2011 to 2026, involving 10,436 unique government organisations. The total declared value of these contracts is ₹3,285,634 Crore. Additionally, 3,952,191 published (open) tenders are tracked.
executive_summary.paragraph_2: The analysis has identified 2 CRITICAL and 2 HIGH severity findings that warrant immediate investigative attention.
executive_summary.paragraph_3: The findings below are ranked by severity. CRITICAL findings represent structural patterns that are inconsistent with fair, transparent procurement. HIGH findings indicate strong statistical anomalies. MEDIUM and LOW findings are patterns that should be monitored but may have legitimate explanations.

findings.title: 500 Contracts Awarded Implausibly Fast (≤ 1 day)
findings.summary: 500 contracts were awarded within 24 hours of the bidding deadline closing.
findings.explanation: Standard procurement procedure requires time to receive bids, evaluate them against technical and financial criteria, obtain approval from a tender committee, and issue the Award of Contract. This process typically takes days to weeks. Yet 500 contracts in this dataset were awarded on the same day — or within 24 hours — of the bidding deadline. This is physically implausible under fair procurement rules.
findings.what_it_means: Contracts awarded this fast were almost certainly pre-decided before bidding opened. The tender process may have been a formality to create a paper trail for a pre-arranged award. This is one of the strongest structural red flags for procurement fraud.
next_steps: Prioritise these contracts for RTI requests — ask for the bid evaluation committee minutes.
Check if the same vendor appears across multiple 'quick award' contracts.
Verify if the award date and bid closing date in the raw data are accurate — some may be data entry errors.
Cross-reference with the vendor who won — are they politically connected?

findings.title: 50 Departments Graded 'F' for Procurement Risk
findings.summary: 50 organisations scored the highest risk level based on single-bid and round-number patterns.
findings.explanation: The risk grading system assigns 'F' to departments where over 40% of contracts show procurement red flags (single bids, round-number values). 50 departments have earned this lowest grade. The highest-volume 'F' grade departments include: BHEL EDN, Indian Space Research Organisation||UR Rao Satellite Centre Bengaluru, Telegana. Together, the top 5 'F' departments control at least ₹8660 Crore in contracts.
findings.what_it_means: An 'F' grade does not automatically mean corruption — it means the procurement patterns match the profile of high-risk procurement. It could reflect poor procedure, weak oversight, or active manipulation. Without investigation, the cause is unknown.
next_steps: Prioritise these 50 departments for RTI requests and audit scrutiny.
Check if 'F' grade departments are concentrated in specific states or sectors.
Compare 'F' departments' single-bid rates against their market — are they justified by specialisation?

worst_orgs: Indian Space Research Organisation||UR Rao Satellite Centre Bengaluru
Bharat Heavy Electricals Limited||BHEL-Power Sector Southern Regions, Chennai||PSSR

findings.title: 500 High-Value State Contracts Over ₹10 Crore
findings.summary: 500 contracts awarded by state governments exceed ₹10 Crore each.
findings.explanation: State government contracts above ₹10 Crore are significant expenditures that should be subject to rigorous oversight, competitive tendering, and approval at senior government levels. This dataset contains 500 such contracts, collectively representing a large share of state procurement spending.
findings.what_it_means: High-value state contracts are particularly prone to political interference in vendor selection, inflated valuations, and poor quality delivery. They are also harder to scrutinize as state-level RTI responses are often slower.
next_steps: Identify which states dominate this list.
Check if any single vendor appears across multiple high-value state contracts.
Look for contracts awarded near state election dates.

findings.title: Vendor 'TASKA FIBRES PRIVATE LIMITED' Won 2124 Contracts from Same Department
findings.summary: A single vendor dominated procurement from one government department with 2124 wins.
findings.explanation: The vendor 'TASKA FIBRES PRIVATE LIMITED' won 2124 contracts from 'BHEL EDN' between 01-Dec-2017 09:00 AM and 31-Oct-2017 09:00 AM, totalling approximately ₹0.0 Crore. This extreme concentration of contracts in one vendor-department relationship is a significant structural anomaly.
findings.what_it_means: Legitimate repeat wins are possible for vendors providing ongoing services or specialised equipment. However, 2124 wins from the same department suggests either a monopoly arrangement, a preferred vendor relationship, or that the tender specifications are written to systematically exclude competitors.
next_steps: Investigate whether 'TASKA FIBRES PRIVATE LIMITED' is the sole supplier in its market or if competitors exist.
Check if any single-bid contracts are included in these wins — the strongest red flag.
Look at the contract values over time — are they increasing? A rising trend suggests dependency.
File RTI to see tender evaluation reports for the highest-value wins.

findings.title: 500 Contracts at Suspiciously Round Numbers
findings.summary: 500 contracts have values that are exact multiples of ₹1 Lakh (₹1,00,000).
findings.explanation: In competitive procurement, contract values should reflect the actual cost of work or goods — a messy, specific number. When 500 contracts are awarded at exactly ₹10,00,000 or ₹50,00,000 or ₹1,00,00,000, it strongly suggests the price was estimated rather than competitively determined. This is a classic signal of single-vendor negotiations or rubber-stamped approvals.
findings.what_it_means: Round-number contracts suggest either: (1) the 'lowest bidder' was told what price to quote, (2) the contract value was set before bidding began, or (3) estimates are being passed off as market-tested prices.
next_steps: Compare round-number contracts against similar contracts with specific values — which orgs have higher rates?
Check if round-number contracts cluster around specific thresholds that trigger higher oversight (e.g., ₹1 Crore).
A high rate of round numbers combined with a high rate of single bids is a very strong compound signal.

findings.title: Top 5 Departments Award 60.9% of All Contracts
findings.summary: Procurement is heavily concentrated in a handful of organisations.
findings.explanation: The top 5 awarding organisations account for 60.9% of all contracts in this dataset. These include: West Bengal, Maharashtra, Kerala, and others. While large infrastructure ministries naturally award more contracts, this level of concentration means the actions of a few departments have outsized impact on overall procurement quality and integrity.
findings.what_it_means: High concentration means a small number of procurement officers control enormous budgets. It also means that if corruption exists in even one of these top departments, its effect on public money is multiplied.
next_steps: Focus auditing and RTI efforts on the top 5 departments first — highest impact per investigation.
Compare the transparency grade (risk score) of high-volume departments vs. their size.

findings.title: 0.1% of Contracts Had Only One Bidder (2,000 contracts)
findings.summary: 2,000 contracts received exactly one bid — meaning there was no competitive pricing.
findings.explanation: Of the contracts in this dataset where bid count data is available, 2,000 (0.1%) received only a single bid. In public procurement, competition is the primary mechanism to ensure taxpayers get fair value for money. A single bid means that mechanism failed completely.
findings.what_it_means: A single-bid rate of 0.1% is present. While some level of single-bid contracts is normal for specialised or highly technical procurements, this rate warrants monitoring to ensure tenders are reaching the widest possible audience.
next_steps: Identify which departments have the highest single-bid rates — these are the highest-risk units.
Check if repeat single-bid contracts repeatedly go to the same vendor.
Look at the tender publication timeline — were single-bid tenders published for unusually short durations?
Cross-reference single-bid contracts with round-number values — the compound signal is very strong.

findings.title: Spending Drop of -54% Between 2025 and 2026
findings.summary: Total contract value fell sharply from ₹362414 Cr to ₹167125 Cr.
findings.explanation: Contract spending dropped by 54% between 2025 and 2026. This could reflect policy changes, budget cuts, or — importantly — a shift to procurement channels not captured in this dataset.
findings.what_it_means: Sharp drops can mean real austerity, but can also mean spending moved to a different procurement portal or mechanism that isn't included in this data.
next_steps: Check if the drop year coincides with new procurement portals being introduced.
Verify coverage of this dataset for the drop year.

findings.title: Spending Drop of -100% Between 2026 and 2027
findings.summary: Total contract value fell sharply from ₹167125 Cr to ₹5 Cr.
findings.explanation: Contract spending dropped by 100% between 2026 and 2027. This could reflect policy changes, budget cuts, or — importantly — a shift to procurement channels not captured in this dataset.
findings.what_it_means: Sharp drops can mean real austerity, but can also mean spending moved to a different procurement portal or mechanism that isn't included in this data.
next_steps: Check if the drop year coincides with new procurement portals being introduced.
Verify coverage of this dataset for the drop year.
